title: Basics of soldering in Biological and Agricultural Engineering Labs (BAEL)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"dqaoo","text":"This protocol describes the basic principles of soldering. An ANBES soldering station is used for this protocol, but most of the methods are interchangeable with other soldering irons. The process requires approximately 30 min, but timing can vary depending on the number/type of components which require soldering. A list of useful references is provided for further reading.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"e0jc6","text":"Eye protection","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ark0t","text":"Laboratory eye protection is required to avoid accidental splashes of hot solder.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7jbrn","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6uj25","text":"Skin","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4045a","text":"Avoid contact of hot metals/solder with skin. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"md0a","text":"Proper use of PPE (lab coat, closed toed shoes) should avoid any problems","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3locu","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vrim","text":"Fumes/aerosols","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fbc0v","text":"All soldering should be conducted in a chemical hood to avoid flux and solder fumes","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"allmf","text":"When a chemical hood is not available, a fume extractor (with activated carbon filter) is Acid core solder is commonly sold at home improvement stores, and is used for plumbing and other types of metal working.","type":"unstyled"}],"entityMap":{}}},"3":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"D342DF78680811EFA4880A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://www.jameco.com/z/209074-R-1-Pound-Lead-Free-Solder-Roll-99-3Sn-0-7Cu-0-031-Inch-Diameter_209075.html"}},"4":{"type":"notes","mutability":"IMMUTABLE","data":{"blocks":[{"data":{},"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"26t7f","text":"Thicker solder (diameter \u003e 0.06″) is applicable for soldering larger joints","type":"unstyled"},{"data":{},"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"3s1o4","text":"Smaller solder (diameter \u003c 0.04”) is effective for smaller joints or circuit boards","type":"unstyled"}],"entityMap":{}}}}}
steps: Wet sponge with DI water and place in soldering iron stand If using a brass sponge it is not necessary to wet with DI water Ensure that soldering iron stand is properly positioned ( Fig 1 ) Figure 1 . Ensure solder stand is correctly arranged. A) Incorrect position, B) Correct position Before touching end of sodering iron, check that soldering iron is OFF and the surface is NOT hot. If unsure, use a non-contact (laser) thermometer to check the temperature of the soldering iron tip prior to handling Inspect the soldering iron tip. The tip should be free of physical bends/scrapes, glue, or other damage Check soldering iron tip to ensure it is secure (hand tighten threaded rod if necessary) ( Fig 2 ) Figure 2 . A) Inspect the soldering iron tip, B) Screw the tip into the iron and make sure it is hand tight Ensure ample tabletop space (or chemical hood space) is available for soldering Arrange materials to be soldered near the soldering iron station (e.g., in the chemical hood)

Tin the tip Turn on the soldering iron If using an adjustable temperature solder station, set the temperature to 330–370C depending on solder type and joint size Wipe the tip of the soldering iron on the wet solder sponge Rotate it back and forth and wipe it at least three times on the surface Wait 10-15 seconds to let the tip heat up again Hold the soldering iron in one hand and solder in the other. Touch the solder to the tip of the iron and make sure the solder flows evenly around the tip. The tip is now “tinned” and ready to use If necessary, use the desoldering pump to remove waste material

Insert components, or arrange as needed If working on a circuit board, mount the component to be soldered by placing metal pins into circuit board holes. Then flip the board and bend the metal pins at a 45 degree angle If a different arrangement/geometry is required, position the items to be soldered next to each other so that they are touching and stable Use of “Helping Hands” is often suggested for this step

Heat joint where solder is to be applied Using a tinned soldering iron that is preheated to 400C, touch the tip of the iron to the joint Hold the iron in place for at least 5-10 seconds to heat the joint and any components For older solder irons (or damaged tips), the heating process can require up to 30 seconds (or sometimes more)

Apply solder to joint While continuing to hold the soldering iron on joint, touch the solder to the joint. IMPORTANT – During joint formation, apply solder primarily to the heated joint rather than melting large amounts directly on the iron tip. It is important that the joint/components are hot enough to melt the solder, otherwise a good electrical connection will not be formed After the solder melts, carefully pull the soldering iron from the tip and place in the stand Wait at least 10-20 seconds after step 5, IMPORTANT – Do not cool the solder, allow it to naturally reach ambient temperature to ensure a good electrical connection

Strip excess solder from leads A proper solder joint is smooth, shiny and has a cone shape. The amount of solder should be sufficient to cover the joint, but not overlap with nearby components Remove any excess material from the joint with anti-static tweezers Optionally, a solder sucker may be used to vacuum hot solder from the surface (either excess solder, or at times to start the process again with a clean surface)

Clean up laboratory Turn off all equipment After the soldering iron has cooled, clean the tip using a stainless steel sponge After removing debris, ensure iron is fully cooled and then clean the tip with a cloth dipped in 70% alcohol Clean workstation/benchtop Dispose of all materials per hazard/warning section
creator: Eric S S McLamore
